content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 15, 2006

Nicole G. Paquette, Esq.
Director of Legal and Government Affairs
Animal Protection Institute
P. O. Box 22505
Sacramento, CA 95822

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-208

Dear Ms. Paquette:

This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance on behalf of the Protect Pets and Wildlife ('ProPAW') Committee regarding the use of funds provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Since your request is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).²

Also your facts suggest that there is the possible application of Election Code section 18680 (copy enclosed), which imposes duties on those entrusted with funds directed at the promotion of ballot measures. The Commission cannot offer advice on bodies of law outside the Act, and we, therefore, strongly recommend that you consult the Attorney General's office regarding the possible application of Election Code section 18680 with regard to ProPAW's use of campaign funds.

QUESTION

May the ProPAW Committee, a state ballot measure committee, use a portion or all of its current surplus cash after an election to:

(1) reimburse three groups that funded the lawsuit to defend Proposition 4;

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. 1-06-208
Page No. 2

(2) fund wildlife-related activities targeted by the Proposition 4 ballot initiative, or
any animal issue;

(3) donate to candidates running for state, federal or local political office; or

(4) hire a lobbyist to pass a bill relating to wildlife activities in general, or must it
be specific to trapping and poisoning of animals?

CONCLUSION

So long as the expenditures are reasonably related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose, ProPAW may use excess campaign funds held after an election
to: (1) reimburse the three groups that funded the lawsuit to defend Proposition 4;
(2) fund wildlife-related activities targeted by the Proposition 4 ballot initiative, or any
other animal issue; (3) donate to candidates running for state, federal or local political
office; or (4) hire a lobbyist to pass a bill relating to wildlife activities in general, or bills
specific to trapping and poisoning of animals.

Please note that the advice given here presumes compliance with the fundamental
obligations of Election Code Section 18660. We cannot render advice with respect to
interpretation of the Elections Code. However, we have advised that to the extent that
the Elections Code and the personal use provisions of the Act can be read together, that is the construction that should be applied. However, if there is a
conflict between the provisions, it is our position that the personal use provisions of the
Act would control over conflicting provisions of the Elections Code.

FACTS

In 1998, California voters passed Proposition 4, a state ballot initiative passed by
a coalition of seven groups called Protect Pets and Wildlife ("ProPAW"). The
proposition prohibited the use of body-gripping traps for recreational and commerce, and
the use of the poison commonly known as Compound 1080 and sodium cyanide. A body-
gripping trap is one that grips the mammal's body or body part, including, but not limited
to, steel-jawed leghold traps, padded-jaw leghold traps, conibear traps, and snares.

Your organization, The Animal Protection Institute ("API"), holds the ProPAW
account and is charged with filing state-mandated reports. The initiative has been the
subject of a lawsuit since its passage in 1998. The lawsuit continued for over six years.
Three groups in the Coalition expended over $100,000 in legal fees defending the ballot
initiative in court.

The court proceeding has concluded and no additional litigation can be brought to
challenge the proposition. The ProPAW bank account currently has more than $70,000.
API would like to close that account and officially terminate the filing requirements.
You also wish to receive advice regarding the use of remaining funds in the ProPAW
account.

File No. I-06-208
Page No. 3

In our telephone conversation on November 30, 2006, you stated that you wished
to return contributions from the three groups (API, the Humane Society of the United
States, and the Fund for Animals) to ProPAW. These contributions were used to support
Proposition 4 and defend the proposition from legal challenges. You also said that the
contemplated expenditures would likely include donations to tax-exempt, nonprofit
organizations. In addition, you indicated that no person with authority to approve the
expenditure of the campaign funds at ProPAW receive a substantial personal benefit from
any of the contemplated expenditures.

ANALYSIS

The Act contains specific provisions governing the use of campaign funds.
(Sections 89510-89522.) These sections are generally referred to as the "personal use"
provisions.

In general, the "personal use" provisions of the Act were designed to prevent
candidates, elected officials, and others who control the expenditures of campaign funds
from benefiting privately from their campaign activities.

Most of the "personal use" provisions are applicable to both candidates and
committees, including statewide ballot measure committees. (Section 89511.) However,
the personal use statutes do not specifically address the permissible uses for funds held by
a ballot measure committee after an election. The only provision that specifically refers
to leftover or "surplus" funds is section 89519, which relates to the disposition of funds
held by candidates rather than by ballot measure committees. (See Hiltachk Advice
Letter, No. I-90-053.) Therefore, we have advised that a ballot measure committee may
use its funds for any purpose permitted by sections 89510 - 89518. (See Lilyquist Advice
Letter, No. I-91-272; Pirayou Advice Letter, No. A-04-221, enclosed.)

The general rule is that an expenditure of campaign funds must be reasonably
related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose. However, where an
expenditure of campaign funds confers a substantial personal benefit on any individual or
individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by the
committee, the expenditure must be directly related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. (Section 89512.5.)

You indicated in our telephone conversation on November 30, 2006, that no
person with authority to approve the expenditure of the campaign funds at ProPAW
would receive a substantial personal benefit.³

³ Regulation 18960 defines a "direct personal benefit" as follows: "(a) For purposes of
Section 89511 and Code section 89511(b)(3), an expenditure of campaign funds results in a direct personal
benefit when, within six months of the expenditure and without the assistance of any intervening influence
or interruption, the candidate or elected officer or member of his or her immediate family: (1) Realizes an
increase in his or her income or assets, or a decrease in his or her expenses or liabilities, of more than $ 200
from the expenditure; or (2) Actually makes personal use of an asset obtained as a result of the
expenditure...."

File No. 1-06-208
Page No. 4

Section 89522 of the Act states that "This chapter shall not be construed to permit
an expenditure of campaign funds prohibited by Section 18680 of the Elections Code."
As previously indicated, we cannot render advice with respect to interpretation of the
Elections Code. Our advice is limited to application of the provisions of the Act.

However, certain general considerations should be kept in mind in analyzing the
interrelationship of the two schemes. Applying general rules of statutory construction,
statutes should be harmonized whenever possible. (In re White (1969) 1 Cal. 3d 207,
212.) Thus, to the extent that the provisions of Elections Code section 18680 and the
personal use provisions of the Act can be read together, that is the construction which
should be applied. However, if there is a conflict between the provisions, it is our
position that the personal use provisions of the Act would control over conflicting
provisions of the Elections Code. (Lilyguist Advice Letter, No. I-91-310 (enclosed);
Section 81013.)

Section 81013 of the Act, provides in pertinent part, that "If any act of the
Legislature conflicts with the provisions of this title, this title shall prevail."

With the foregoing in mind, we provide you with the following analysis of the
specific questions posed by your request.

Return of Contributions

The personal use laws do not provide specific guidance regarding the refund of
contributions to contributors. We have, therefore, advised that such expenditures may be
made so long as the refund is reasonably related to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. (Bell Advice Letter, No. A-00-010; Hiltachk Advice Letter,
No. I-90-053.)

According to your facts, the Proposition 4 initiative has been the subject of a
lawsuit challenging its implementation since its passage in 1998, and that three groups
contributed more than $100,000 to the ProPAW committee that was used for legal fees in
defending the ballot initiative in court. You wish to use some of the remaining funds to
return a portion of the contributions made by these groups.

The reasons you described for the return of the groups' contributions appear
reasonably related to a political purpose, and would, therefore, be a permissible
expenditure.

Funding of Wildlife-related Activities:

You did not include any details regarding specific expenditures that the
committee is contemplating with regard to support of wildlife activities related to
Proposition 4, or general animal issues. As stated previously, the expenditures would be
permissible so long as they bear a reasonable relationship to political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. You did indicate that the contemplated expenditures would likely
include donations to tax-exempt, nonprofit organizations. Thus, specific requirements
must also be met.

File No. 1-06-208
Page No. 5

The Act provides that a ballot measure committee may use its campaign funds to
make donations to nonprofit organizations provided that three requirements are met.
First, the recipient organization must be a bona fide charitable, educational, civic,
religious, or similar tax-exempt nonprofit organization. Second, no substantial part of the
proceeds of the donation can have a material financial effect on a candidate or elected
officer, members of his or her immediate family or campaign treasurer, or any individual
or individuals with authority to approve the expenditure of campaign funds held by a
committee. Third, the donation must bear a reasonable relationship to a political,
legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89515.) Therefore, the contemplated
expenditures would be permissible if they meet the above criteria.

Other Expenditures:

You did not include any details regarding contemplated expenditures related to
the hiring of a lobbyist to pass a bill relating to wildlife activities, or the donation of
funds to candidates running for federal, state, or local political office.

Generally speaking, ProPAW may use excess campaign funds for these purposes
so long as the expenditures bear a reasonable relationship to a political, legislative, or
governmental purpose. Whether these expenditures bear a reasonable relationship to a
political, legislative, or governmental purpose depends on the particular facts of a case.

For instance, we have advised that a committee may make expenditures of
campaign funds only for legislative monitoring and advocacy services where the
services are directly related to a political purpose. (Brown Advice Letter, No. I-90-412,
enclosed.)

We have also advised that a local ballot measure committee may contribute funds
to politicians who supported the committee's position in an election because
contributions to candidates, who shared the committee's view on the ballot measure
issue, would constitute an expenditure that is reasonably related to a political purpose of the
committee. (Denis Advice Letter, A-94-286, enclosed.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures
